text:
HEMATOLOGY-ONCOLOGY INPATIENT CONSULT NOTE

ATTENDING: [Hematology-Oncology]
CONSULT SERVICE: Hematology-Oncology
REQUESTING SERVICE: Internal Medicine / Primary Care
REASON FOR CONSULT: Acute leukocytosis with circulating blasts, thrombocytopenia, anemia -- urgent hematologic evaluation and management

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT:
Ms. Renata Vasquez-Okonkwo is a 48-year-old female presenting with a 5-week history of worsening fatigue, exertional shortness of breath, easy bruising, and bleeding gums. She was found to have a WBC of 87,400/uL with an estimated 42% circulating blasts on peripheral smear and was referred emergently for hematology-oncology evaluation.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
Ms. Vasquez-Okonkwo is a 48-year-old female biology teacher with a background of well-controlled hypothyroidism and mild hypertension who presents with a several-week history of a progressive systemic illness that has substantially impaired her functioning. She reports that approximately 5 weeks ago she began noticing unusually severe fatigue -- described as bone-deep exhaustion -- which she initially attributed to occupational stress near the end of the school term. Over the subsequent weeks, she developed increasing shortness of breath with exertion, noting that she becomes winded climbing a single flight of stairs, an activity she previously performed without difficulty. She has noted easy bruising over minor trauma sites, as well as spontaneous bleeding from her gums while brushing her teeth, which has been persistent and difficult to control. She reports drenching night sweats requiring a change of bedclothes on several occasions per week. She has had an unintentional weight loss of approximately 7 pounds over the past 6 weeks without dietary modification and without obvious explanation.

She presented to her primary care physician today for evaluation. On examination there, she was found to be pale and mildly diaphoretic. Splenomegaly was noted on palpation, approximately 3 cm below the left costal margin. Multiple petechiae were observed on the bilateral lower extremities and soft palate. There was active oozing from the gingival margins. A CBC obtained urgently at the primary care office revealed a WBC of 87,400/uL, hemoglobin of 7.2 g/dL, and platelet count of 28,000/uL. Peripheral blood smear was reviewed and demonstrated circulating blasts estimated at approximately 42% of white cell differential. She was referred directly to our hematology-oncology service and has been admitted for urgent evaluation.

She denies headache, visual changes, confusion, focal neurologic symptoms, chest pain, hemoptysis, hematuria, melena, or hematochezia. She has not had fevers prior to today's presentation. She denies prior episodes of similar symptoms. She has no known prior hematologic disorders. She is a non-smoker and reports no significant prior chemical or occupational exposures. She has not been taking NSAIDs or anticoagulants.

---

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY:
1. Hypothyroidism -- managed with levothyroxine 75 mcg daily; TSH last checked approximately 8 months ago, within normal limits per patient report
2. Mild hypertension -- previously treated with lifestyle modification; recently started on low-dose antihypertensive (see medications)
3. No prior malignancy
4. No prior hematologic disorders
5. No prior radiation therapy or chemotherapy exposure
6. Spontaneous vaginal delivery x2, no obstetric complications

SURGICAL HISTORY:
- Appendectomy, age 22 (uncomplicated)
- No other prior surgeries

---

SOCIAL HISTORY:
Ms. Vasquez-Okonkwo works full-time as a high school biology teacher. She is married with two adult children. She is a lifelong non-smoker. She reports occasional social alcohol use (1-2 drinks per week, less than prior to symptom onset). She denies illicit drug use. She denies recreational cannabis use. She denies any occupational or environmental chemical exposures of significance (no pesticide, benzene, or radiation exposure identified). She was born outside the United States; country of origin not specified in current records. She lives with her husband in the area. She has a strong support system at home.

---

FAMILY HISTORY:
- Mother: Type 2 diabetes mellitus, hypertension; alive
- Father: Deceased, cause unknown to patient
- One brother: alive, no known malignancy
- No known family history of leukemia, lymphoma, myelodysplastic syndrome, or other hematologic malignancy
- No known family history of inherited bone marrow failure syndromes (Fanconi anemia, dyskeratosis congenita, Diamond-Blackfan anemia)
- No known family history of breast, ovarian, or colon cancer

---

ALLERGIES:
- Penicillin: reported rash (nature and timing unclear; documented as allergy in primary care records)
- No other known drug allergies
- No known food allergies

---

MEDICATIONS (on admission):
1. Levothyroxine 75 mcg orally every morning on an empty stomach
2. Amlodipine 5 mg orally daily (for hypertension)
3. Multivitamin daily
4. No anticoagulants, no antiplatelets, no NSAIDs
5. No prior immunosuppressants or chemotherapy

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS:
Positive for: profound fatigue, exertional dyspnea, easy bruising, spontaneous gingival bleeding, night sweats, unintentional weight loss (~7 lbs/6 weeks), pallor noted by family.
Negative for: fever (prior to today), chills, headache, visual disturbances, confusion, altered mental status, nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain, diarrhea, constipation, hematuria, melena, hematochezia, hemoptysis, chest pain, palpitations, syncope, focal neurologic deficits, bone pain, joint pain, rash, oral ulcers (aside from bleeding gums), lymph node swelling noted by patient.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:
Vital signs: Temperature 37.1°C, heart rate 102 bpm (sinus tachycardia), blood pressure 118/74 mmHg, respiratory rate 18/min, oxygen saturation 97% on room air. Weight: 67 kg. Height: 163 cm. BMI: 25.2 kg/m².

General: Pale-appearing female in mild distress, tired-appearing, in no acute respiratory distress. Alert and oriented x3. Somewhat anxious.

HEENT: Pale conjunctival mucosa bilaterally. Anicteric sclerae. Oropharynx: multiple petechiae on the soft palate; active oozing from the gingival margins noted at the time of examination. No ulcerations of the buccal mucosa or tongue. No tonsillar enlargement.

Neck: Supple. No cervical, submandibular, or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy appreciated.

Chest/Pulmonary: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheeze, rhonchi, or crackles.

Cardiovascular: Tachycardic at 102 bpm. Regular rhythm. Normal S1 and S2. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No jugular venous distension.

Abdomen: Soft, non-tender to palpation. Splenomegaly: spleen palpable approximately 3 cm below the left costal margin on deep inspiration, smooth surface, non-tender. No hepatomegaly appreciable on palpation. No ascites.

Extremities: Multiple petechiae scattered over bilateral lower extremities from the ankles to the thighs. Ecchymoses of varying ages noted over bilateral forearms and shins. No peripheral edema. Pulses 2+ throughout.

Skin: Pallor. Petechiae as described. No jaundice. No rash.

Neurologic: Alert and oriented. Cranial nerves II-XII grossly intact. Sensation grossly intact. Motor strength preserved in all four extremities. No focal deficits.

Lymph nodes: No axillary, inguinal, or epitrochlear lymphadenopathy appreciated.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS:
CBC (from primary care office, confirmed on repeat draw at admission):
- WBC: 87,400/uL (markedly elevated)
- Hemoglobin: 7.2 g/dL (severely reduced)
- Hematocrit: 21.6%
- Platelet count: 28,000/uL (severely thrombocytopenic)
- Peripheral blood smear: marked leukocytosis, circulating blasts ~42% of differential, few mature granulocytes, relative monocytopenia; Auer rods noted in a subset of blast-appearing cells (to be confirmed on formal morphology review by hematopathology)

Comprehensive metabolic panel (preliminary):
- Sodium: 138 mEq/L
- Potassium: 3.9 mEq/L
- Creatinine: 0.78 mg/dL (normal)
- BUN: 14 mg/dL
- Total bilirubin: 1.1 mg/dL
- AST: 34 U/L, ALT: 29 U/L (mildly elevated upper limits noted)
- LDH: 1,842 U/L (markedly elevated, consistent with high tumor burden and rapid cell turnover)
- Uric acid: 8.6 mg/dL (elevated, concerning for impending tumor lysis)
- Phosphorus: 5.1 mg/dL (mildly elevated)
- Calcium: 9.1 mg/dL (normal)

Coagulation studies:
- PT/INR: 1.3 / 13.9 seconds (mildly prolonged)
- PTT: 36.2 seconds (borderline prolonged)
- Fibrinogen: 168 mg/dL (low-normal; DIC screen in progress)
- D-dimer: pending

Blood type and screen: sent
CMV serology: sent
HLA typing: sent (in anticipation of possible transplant evaluation)
Hepatitis B surface antigen, hepatitis B core antibody (total), hepatitis C antibody: sent
HIV: sent
Fungal serologies (Aspergillus galactomannan): deferred for now
TSH: pending

---

IMAGING:
CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast (performed today): Significant splenomegaly at 16.4 cm craniocaudal dimension with homogeneous parenchyma. Mild hepatomegaly (liver span 17.1 cm). No discrete hepatic lesions. No lymphadenopathy above or below the diaphragm. No pleural effusions, pericardial effusion, or pulmonary infiltrates/masses. No obvious extramedullary disease identified.

ECG (obtained for baseline cardiac assessment): Sinus tachycardia at 102 bpm. Normal axis. No ST changes. QTc 412 ms.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN:

Ms. Vasquez-Okonkwo is a 48-year-old female presenting with an acute illness characterized by severe anemia, marked thrombocytopenia, leukocytosis with circulating blasts (approximately 42% on peripheral smear), and constitutional symptoms including night sweats and significant weight loss. The clinical picture -- together with Auer rods on smear, splenomegaly, and the immunophenotypic features to be formally evaluated -- is strongly consistent with acute myeloid leukemia (AML), most likely de novo given no prior history of myelodysplastic syndrome, prior chemotherapy, or radiation therapy.

The differential at this time includes acute myeloid leukemia (most likely given Auer rod morphology, myeloid blast appearance, and flow cytometry pending), acute lymphoblastic leukemia (less likely given blast morphology and absence of lymphadenopathy/mediastinal mass), high-grade myeloid neoplasm arising from MDS (possible but no prior diagnosis), or a blastic transformation of a chronic myeloid process (less consistent with presentation).

1. URGENT BONE MARROW BIOPSY AND ASPIRATE
   - Bone marrow biopsy and aspirate scheduled urgently for tomorrow morning
   - Aspirate sent for: morphology, flow cytometry immunophenotyping, cytogenetics (conventional karyotype + FISH panel for AML-associated translocations including t(8;21), inv(16)/t(16;16), t(15;17), and MLL/KMT2A rearrangements), and molecular panel (FLT3-ITD, FLT3-TKD, NPM1, CEBPA, IDH1/IDH2, RUNX1, ASXL1; NGS panel to follow if adequate material)
   - Core biopsy for histology and immunohistochemistry

2. TUMOR LYSIS SYNDROME PROPHYLAXIS
   - Allopurinol 300 mg orally daily initiated today
   - Vigorous IV hydration with normal saline (at least 150-200 mL/hr initially)
   - Serial monitoring of BMP, uric acid, phosphorus, LDH, and calcium every 6-8 hours overnight and through initial workup period
   - Rasburicase on standby given markedly elevated LDH and uric acid at presentation

3. TRANSFUSION SUPPORT
   - Hemoglobin 7.2 g/dL -- transfusion threshold in this setting given symptomatic anemia; will transfuse 2 units pRBC tonight with target Hgb > 8 g/dL
   - Platelet count 28,000/uL with active mucosal bleeding -- will transfuse 1 pool platelets now; maintain platelets > 20,000/uL during active bleeding, > 10,000/uL otherwise
   - All blood products to be irradiated and leukoreduced
   - CMV seronegative products requested pending CMV serologic results

4. COAGULOPATHY / BLEEDING RISK
   - DIC screen (fibrinogen, D-dimer) in progress; PT/INR mildly prolonged, fibrinogen borderline low -- monitoring closely
   - If DIC is confirmed, will consider cryoprecipitate to maintain fibrinogen > 150 mg/dL
   - Gingival bleeding currently present; patient instructed on careful oral hygiene, chlorhexidine rinses ordered

5. PENICILLIN ALLERGY DOCUMENTATION
   - Allergy listed as penicillin with rash; will document and avoid beta-lactams unless allergy fully evaluated; cephalosporins and carbapenems available if antimicrobial therapy is needed (with monitoring given cross-reactivity)

6. INDUCTION CHEMOTHERAPY (anticipated pending final diagnosis)
   - Pending bone marrow and molecular results, strong clinical suspicion for AML; plan to initiate standard induction chemotherapy (7+3 regimen with cytarabine and anthracycline) once diagnosis is confirmed and patient is medically stable
   - If FLT3 mutation identified (which will be assessed urgently), will add midostaurin per current standard of care
   - Echocardiogram will be obtained prior to anthracycline administration to document baseline LVEF
   - Detailed informed consent discussion will be conducted with patient and family prior to initiating induction

7. SUPPORTIVE CARE
   - Antimicrobial prophylaxis (fluoroquinolone and antifungal) to be initiated once induction begins
   - Acyclovir for herpes prophylaxis with chemotherapy
   - Strict neutropenic precautions once induction initiated
   - Social work and case management referrals placed
   - Nutrition consult ordered given 7-pound weight loss

8. SOCIAL AND PSYCHOSOCIAL SUPPORT
   - Patient and family counseling regarding preliminary diagnosis, planned workup, and treatment pathway
   - Given her age (48), good performance status, and no significant comorbidities, she is a potential candidate for allogeneic stem cell transplantation if disease risk warrants -- this will be discussed further after molecular results are finalized
   - HLA typing has been sent proactively
   - Social work referral placed

9. MONITORING / FOLLOW-UP
   - Continuous telemetry monitoring while inpatient
   - Vital signs and neuro checks every 4 hours
   - Daily CBCs and CMPs with uric acid and LDH until bone marrow results finalized and chemotherapy plan established
   - Regular communication with primary care physician regarding hospital course

We will follow closely with the patient and will update the plan as diagnostic data become available. The clinical urgency of this presentation is high given the degree of leukocytosis, thrombocytopenia, and active mucosal hemorrhage. The patient has been informed of the seriousness of her condition and is understandably distressed but cooperative and engaged in the diagnostic process.

--- Electronically signed by Attending Hematologist-Oncologist ---
